abstract:
Skill usage has become a core component of modern agent systems and can substantially improve agents' ability to complete complex tasks. In real-world settings, where agents must monitor and interact with numerous personal applications, web browsers, and other environment interfaces, skill libraries can scale to thousands of reusable skills. Scaling to larger skill sets introduces two key challenges. First, loading the full skill set saturates the context window, driving up token costs, hallucination, and latency.
In this paper, we present Graph of Skills (GoS), an inference-time structural retrieval layer for large skill libraries. GoS constructs an executable skill graph offline from skill packages, then at inference time retrieves a bounded, dependency-aware skill bundle through hybrid semantic-lexical seeding, reverse-weighted Personalized PageRank, and context-budgeted hydration. On SkillsBench and ALFWorld, GoS improves average reward by 43.6% over the vanilla full skill-loading baseline while reducing input tokens by 37.8%, and generalizes across three model families: Claude Sonnet, GPT-5.2 Codex, and MiniMax. Additional ablation studies across skill libraries ranging from 200 to 2,000 skills further demonstrate that GoS consistently outperforms both vanilla skills loading and simple vector retrieval in balancing reward, token efficiency, and runtime.

citations.fullCitation: Xukun Liu, Zhiyuan Peng, Xiaoyuan Yi, Xing Xie, Lirong Xiang, Yuchen Liu, and Dongkuan Xu. Toolnet: Connecting large language models with massive tools via tool graph. arXiv preprint arXiv:2403.00839, 2024b.
citations.justification: This paper is highly relevant as it also proposes using a graph structure for tool access. The 'Graph of Skills' paper explicitly contrasts its approach of retrieving a dependency-complete local bundle with ToolNet's objective of connecting models to a broader tool ecosystem for planning.
citations.title: Toolnet: Connecting large language models with massive tools via tool graph

citations.fullCitation: Yujia Qin, Shihao Liang, Yining Ye, Kunlun Zhu, Lan Yan, Yaxi Lu, Yankai Lin, Xin Cong, Xiangru Tang, Bill Qian, Sihan Zhao, Lauren Hong, Runchu Tian, Ruobing Xie, Jie Zhou, Mark Gerstein, Dahai Li, Zhiyuan Liu, and Maosong Sun. Toolllm: Facilitating large language models to master 16000+ real-world apis. In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2024.
citations.justification: ToolLLM establishes the challenge of scaling agent skills to massive libraries with over 16,000 APIs, which is the core problem that 'Graph of Skills' is designed to solve. The GoS paper uses work like ToolLLM to motivate the need for an efficient and dependency-aware retrieval layer.
citations.title: Toolllm: Facilitating large language models to master 16000+ real-world apis

citations.fullCitation: Yuan Liang, Ruobin Zhong, Haoming Xu, Chen Jiang, Yi Zhong, Runnan Fang, Jia-Chen Gu, Shumin Deng, Yunzhi Yao, Mengru Wang, Shuofei Qiao, Xin Xu, Tongtong Wu, Kun Wang, Yang Liu, Zhen Bi, Jungang Lou, Yuchen Eleanor Jiang, Hangcheng Zhu, Gang Yu, Haiwen Hong, Longtao Huang, Hui Xue, Chenxi Wang, Yijun Wang, Zifei Shan, Xi Chen, Zhaopeng Tu, Feiyu Xiong, Xin Xie, Peng Zhang, Zhengke Gui, Lei Liang, Jun Zhou, Chiyu Wu, Jin Shang, Yu Gong, Junyu Lin, Changliang Xu, Hongjie Deng, Wen Zhang, Keyan Ding, Qiang Zhang, Fei Huang, Ningyu Zhang, Jeff Z. Pan, Guilin Qi, Haofen Wang, and Huajun Chen. Skillnet: Create, evaluate, and connect ai skills. arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.04448, 2026.
citations.justification: The paper positions 'Graph of Skills' as a complementary component for large-scale skill ecosystems like SkillNet. While SkillNet focuses on the creation and organization of skills at a repository level, GoS addresses the critical problem of retrieving a relevant, executable subset from such a library for a given task.
citations.title: Skillnet: Create, evaluate, and connect ai skills

citations.fullCitation: Zhengliang Shi, Yuhan Wang, Lingyong Yan, Pengjie Ren, Shuaiqiang Wang, Dawei Yin, and Zhaochun Ren. Retrieval models aren’t tool-savvy: Benchmarking tool retrieval for large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.01763, 2025.
citations.justification: This citation provides the core motivation for the 'Graph of Skills' paper, as it is cited for establishing that tool retrieval is a major bottleneck and that generic retrievers are poorly suited for real tool-use needs. This directly justifies the development of GoS's dependency-aware, structural retrieval approach.
citations.title: Retrieval models aren’t tool-savvy: Benchmarking tool retrieval for large language models

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Graph of Skills: Dependency-Aware Structural Retrieval for Massive Agent Skills."

---

### Report on "Graph of Skills: Dependency-Aware Structural Retrieval for Massive Agent Skills"

#### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors from several academic institutions:
*   Dawei Li: University of Pennsylvania
*   Zongxia Li: University of Maryland
*   Hongyang Du: Brown University
*   Xiyang Wu: University of Maryland
*   Shihang Gui: Brown University
*   Yongbei Kuang: Carnegie Mellon University
*   Lichao Sun: Lehigh University

#### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The development of Large Language Model (LLM) agents capable of solving complex technical tasks has increasingly relied on their ability to invoke external tools, APIs, and reusable skills. As these skill libraries expand from a few dozen to thousands or even tens of thousands of candidates, the primary challenge transitions from deciding when to use a skill to efficiently retrieving the most relevant and sufficient set of skills for a given task. This shift introduces significant bottlenecks in realistic tool ecosystems.

Current strategies for handling large skill libraries include:
1.  **Vanilla Skills (Full Loading):** This approach involves prepending the entire skill set to the LLM's context window. While effective for small toolsets, it scales poorly. Token costs increase linearly with library size, and the LLM can overlook critical domain constraints within an overloaded context, potentially leading to increased hallucination and latency.
2.  **Vector-Based Retrieval:** This method improves efficiency by retrieving skills based on semantic similarity, typically using dense embeddings. However, semantic proximity does not guarantee functional sufficiency. For many engineering tasks, a high-level semantically matched solver might require lower-level parsers, converters, setup utilities, or domain-specific preprocessors that are functionally necessary but semantically weak or distant from the initial query. This limitation is referred to as the "prerequisite gap."

This work, Graph of Skills (GoS), aims to address these limitations. It positions itself as an inference-time structural retrieval layer specifically designed for large *local* skill libraries. GoS complements broader, repository-scale skill infrastructures such as SkillNet and AgentSkillOS, which focus on creating, organizing, evaluating, and orchestrating vast skill ecosystems. Instead of offering only keyword or vector search over repository metadata, GoS focuses on retrieving the smallest and most relevant executable subset of skills, ensuring it is sufficient for the current task by parsing skill specifications into executable fields and leveraging dependency and workflow structures. The approach contributes to the growing area of graph-based retrieval and relational memory in agent systems, distinguishing itself by focusing on recovering dependency-complete executable bundles under tight context budgets, rather than merely synthesizing knowledge or guiding planning.

#### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to enhance the performance and efficiency of LLM agents when operating with massive skill libraries by introducing a novel skill retrieval mechanism. The motivation stems from several critical challenges observed in contemporary LLM agent systems:

1.  **Context Window Saturation and Cost Efficiency:** As skill libraries grow to thousands of entries, loading the entire set into an LLM's context window becomes prohibitively expensive in terms of token usage and computational latency. This also increases the likelihood of "hallucination," where the model generates incorrect responses, and makes it difficult for the LLM to identify and utilize the most relevant information efficiently. The objective is to reduce the input tokens consumed by the agent while preserving or improving task performance.

2.  **Addressing the "Prerequisite Gap" in Semantic Retrieval:** Purely semantic retrieval methods, while token-efficient, frequently fail to retrieve all necessary components for a task. A task often requires a sequence of skills where some are direct semantic matches to the query (e.g., a high-level solver), while others are essential prerequisites (e.g., data parsers, format converters, setup utilities) that are semantically distant from the primary task description. The motivation is to overcome this "prerequisite gap" by ensuring the retrieved skill bundle is not only relevant but also functionally sufficient and dependency-complete for successful execution.

3.  **Improving Skill Selection Accuracy for Complex Tasks:** Many real-world tasks require multi-step execution paths and careful orchestration of various skills. When agents are provided with an incomplete or noisy set of skills, their ability to devise and execute a correct plan diminishes, leading to lower task completion rates or inefficient search behavior. The objective is to improve the agent's average reward (success rate) by providing a more precise and comprehensive skill bundle upfront.

4.  **Developing an Inference-Time Structural Retrieval Layer:** The goal is to create a dynamic retrieval mechanism that can operate efficiently at inference time, avoiding the need for extensive pre-training or fine-tuning for specific skill sets. This involves constructing a structured representation of the skill library offline and leveraging this structure for rapid, dependency-aware retrieval during agent operation.

In summary, the motivation is to enable LLM agents to effectively leverage large skill libraries by developing a retrieval system that provides a compact, dependency-aware, and executable set of skills, thereby improving task success, reducing operational costs, and enhancing agent reliability.

#### 4. Methodology and Approach

Graph of Skills (GoS) is an inference-time retrieval layer designed for large local skill libraries. Its methodology involves a two-phase process: an offline phase for graph construction and an online phase for structural retrieval.

**4.1 Problem Setup**
GoS considers a local corpus of skill packages, `C`, where each package contains a specification and auxiliary assets. This corpus is transformed into a typed directed graph `G = (V, E, w, ϕ)`, where `V` represents normalized executable skill records, `E` represents edges connecting skills, `w(e)` is an edge weight, and `ϕ(e)` assigns an edge type from `R = {dep, wf, sem, alt}` (dependency, workflow, semantic, alternative).
Given a task query `q` and a context budget `τ`, the problem is to return a bundle `B(q) ⊆ V` that is relevant, execution-complete, and compact. GoS approximates this objective through hybrid seed retrieval, reverse-aware graph diffusion, and budgeted reranking plus hydration.

**4.2 Offline Graph Construction**
This phase builds the foundational graph structure from the skill library.
*   **Skill Normalization:** Each skill package is parsed, primarily from its `SKILL.md` file and package structure, into a normalized skill record. This record includes a canonical name, capability summary, I/O fields, domain tags, tooling, entrypoints, compatibility notes, and a stable local source path. This process is largely deterministic, extracting executable fields. If documentation is incomplete, a lightweight LLM pass is optionally used to recover retrieval-critical semantic fields (e.g., capability summaries, I/O, example tasks). This LLM use is constrained to normalize a single skill node, ensuring the output is grounded in package structure rather than unconstrained generation.
*   **Typed Relation Induction:** GoS employs four types of directed edges:
    *   **Dependency (`dep`):** An edge `v_i → v_j` is added if outputs from `v_i` are compatible with inputs required by `v_j`, indicating `v_i` can provide an artifact consumed by `v_j`. These are induced deterministically by I/O matching.
    *   **Workflow (`wf`):** Captures common multi-step pipelines.
    *   **Semantic (`sem`):** Connects near-duplicate or topically adjacent skills.
    *   **Alternative (`alt`):** Links interchangeable strategies for the same subproblem.
    Non-dependency edges are constructed through sparse LLM validation. For each node, a small candidate pool is formed using lexical similarity, semantic neighbors, and I/O-based expansion. Relation validation is then applied only within this pool by an LLM, ensuring tractability and precision in graph construction.

**4.3 Online Structural Retrieval**
This phase leverages the constructed graph to retrieve skills at query time.
*   **Query Representation and Hybrid Seeding:**
    *   The raw task query `q` is optionally mapped to a lightweight retrieval schema containing the task goal, salient operations, referenced artifacts, and normalized keywords. This can be done via an LLM rewrite or deterministic lexical normalization.
    *   For each candidate skill `v_i`, a semantic seed score `s_sem_i(q)` (from dense retrieval, e.g., using `openai/text-embedding-3-large`) and a lexical seed score `s_lex_i(q)` (from probabilistic ranking over normalized node fields) are computed. These scores are merged (weighted by `η`) to form `z_i(q)`, which is then normalized to create the initial seed distribution `p`. This hybrid approach combines the strengths of semantic and lexical search.
*   **Reverse-Aware Typed Diffusion:**
    *   A unified transition operator `T` is constructed. It combines row-normalized forward operators (`T→_r`) and reverse operators (`T←_r`, derived from the transpose of the adjacency matrix) for each relation type `r`. Each relation type contributes with weights `λ_r` and `γ_r`, where `γ_r` controls the strength of reverse traversal.
    *   The core retrieval step involves a Personalized PageRank-style diffusion: `s^(ℓ+1) = α p + (1−α)Tᵀs^(ℓ)`. Here, `α` is the restart parameter. This diffusion propagates relevance not only to individually matched skills but also across their local executable neighborhood. Critically, reverse transitions allow scores to flow from a matched high-level skill back to its likely prerequisites (e.g., parsers, setup skills), even if these prerequisites are not strong semantic matches to the original query.
*   **Budgeted Reranking and Hydration:**
    *   After diffusion, candidate skills are reranked using a score `ρ_i(q)` that combines the converged diffusion score `s*_i` with direct field-level query evidence `m_i(q)` (weighted by `μ`). This ensures local grounding is preserved after graph expansion.
    *   Skills are then "hydrated" (materialized into agent-consumable payloads including source paths and capability text) in descending order of `ρ_i(q)`, subject to per-skill and global context budgets. The final output is a compact, bounded execution bundle designed to maximize executable coverage within the prompt budget.

#### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of GoS involved comparing its performance against two baselines (Vanilla Skills and Vector Skills) across two benchmarks and three different LLM families, measuring average reward, token usage, and runtime.

**5.1 Experimental Setup**
*   **Benchmarks:** SkillsBench (a 1,000-skill dataset of diverse real-world technical tasks across 11 domains) and ALFWorld (a 140-episode text-only interactive environment for sequential decision-making).
*   **Baselines:**
    *   **Vanilla Skills:** Prepends the entire skill library to the agent's context.
    *   **Vector Skills:** Retrieves a bounded set of skills using semantic similarity based on `openai/text-embedding-3-large` (the same embedding model used by GoS).
*   **Models:** Claude Sonnet 4.5, MiniMax M2.7, and GPT-5.2 Codex.
*   **Metrics:** Average reward (success rate for ALFWorld), average total token usage, and agent-only runtime.

**5.2 Main Results (Table 1 Summary)**
GoS consistently achieved the highest average reward across all six model-benchmark combinations.
*   **Performance vs. Vanilla Skills:** GoS improved average reward by 43.6% (mean across benchmarks) while reducing input tokens by 37.8% (mean across benchmarks) compared to the Vanilla Skills baseline. It also reduced agent runtime in 5 out of 6 blocks. For example, on SkillsBench with Claude Sonnet 4.5, GoS achieved 31.0% reward versus Vanilla Skills' 25.0%, with significantly fewer tokens (860,315 vs. 967,791).
*   **Performance vs. Vector Skills:** GoS improved reward in every block compared to Vector Skills, while maintaining token efficiency within the same compressed regime. For instance, on SkillsBench with Claude Sonnet 4.5, GoS achieved 31.0% reward compared to Vector Skills' 19.3%. This suggests that the graph structure successfully addressed the "prerequisite gap" where semantic retrieval alone was insufficient.
*   **Efficiency-Performance Tradeoff:** GoS demonstrated the strongest overall tradeoff, providing superior reward compared to Vector Skills while being significantly more token-efficient and often faster than Vanilla Skills. The paper notes that naïve semantic retrieval (Vector Skills) particularly struggled on long-horizon tasks requiring combinations of skills and prerequisites, a gap GoS consistently closed.

**5.3 Qualitative Analysis**
A qualitative inspection, primarily focusing on the "Pedestrian Traffic Counting" task (Section 4.3) and expanded upon in Appendix F, indicated that GoS did not merely retrieve topically similar skills but exposed a bundle closer to the executable decomposition of the task. For "Pedestrian Traffic Counting," GoS retrieved a compact visual pipeline (e.g., `gemini-count-in-video`, `video-frame-extraction`, `openai-vision`) leading to the highest score. Vanilla Skills eventually found relevant helpers but with more noise, and Vector Skills failed to form a workable plan. This suggests that GoS facilitates earlier commitment to verifier-aligned plans.

**5.4 Ablation Study: Sensitivity to Skill Library Size (Table 2 & Figure 3)**
An additional study on SkillsBench with GPT-5.2 Codex, varying library size from 200 to 2,000 skills, revealed:
*   **Prompt Cost:** Vanilla Skills' token usage grew rapidly (from 1.85M at 200 skills to 5.84M at 2,000 skills). In contrast, Vector Skills and GoS maintained much lower and relatively stable token usage (around 1.1M-1.4M), confirming that retrieval methods substantially weaken the coupling between repository size and prompt size.
*   **Reward Advantage:** GoS maintained a reward advantage over both baselines at all tested scales from 500 to 2,000 skills, with its margin being largest at 1,000 skills. At 200 skills, Vanilla Skills was slightly ahead. This indicates that the benefit of dependency-aware retrieval increases with library size.
*   **Runtime:** For GPT-5.2 Codex, retrieval-based methods (GoS and Vector Skills) were sometimes slower in agent-only runtime than Vanilla Skills, potentially due to internal caching mechanisms for fixed skill libraries within the black-box model. However, for Claude and MiniMax, Vanilla Skills was slower, indicating that the primary bottleneck is often the overhead of exposing a large, flat library.

**5.5 Component Analysis of the GoS Retrieval Pipeline (Table 2)**
An ablation study on GoS components for SkillsBench with GPT-5.2 Codex (1,000 skills) showed:
*   **Graph Propagation:** Removing graph propagation (disabling expansion beyond seed skills) reduced average token usage but lowered average reward by 5.1 points (from 34.4% to 29.3%). This highlights the importance of structural expansion for recovering prerequisites.
*   **Lexical Retrieval and Reranking:** Removing lexical retrieval and reranking reduced average token usage but lowered average reward by 7.7 points (from 34.4% to 26.7%). This suggests that hybrid semantic-lexical seeding and reranking are crucial for generating high-quality initial seeds, which in turn benefits graph expansion.

In summary, GoS demonstrated consistent improvements in task success rates and efficiency over both full-loading and purely semantic retrieval baselines, particularly as skill library size increased and for tasks requiring complex dependencies. The structural awareness and hybrid seeding mechanisms were identified as key contributors to its performance.

#### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The Graph of Skills (GoS) research addresses a significant and growing challenge in the field of LLM agents: effectively managing and utilizing massive libraries of reusable skills. Its findings have several important implications and potential impacts:

1.  **Enabling Scalable Agent Capabilities:** GoS directly tackles the scalability limitations of current LLM agent systems. By significantly reducing token consumption and often runtime while improving task completion rates, it enables agents to operate with skill libraries far larger than previously practical. This unlocks the potential for agents to tackle more complex, real-world tasks that demand a vast array of specialized skills, without being hindered by context window limitations or prohibitive operational costs.

2.  **Improving Agent Reliability and Robustness:** The "prerequisite gap" identified in purely semantic retrieval is a critical failure mode for agents on multi-step tasks. GoS's dependency-aware structural retrieval mechanism directly mitigates this, leading to more complete and functionally sufficient skill bundles. This enhances the reliability and robustness of agents by ensuring they have access to all necessary tools for a task, reducing instances of partial execution or outright failure due to missing dependencies.

3.  **Advancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for Agents:** The work contributes to the broader RAG paradigm by demonstrating a sophisticated retrieval strategy tailored specifically for agentic skill usage. Unlike traditional RAG for knowledge synthesis, GoS focuses on retrieving executable components and their interdependencies, showcasing how structural information can transform retrieval from merely providing relevant documents to assembling an actionable plan.

4.  **Complementary to Skill Ecosystem Development:** GoS is designed to be complementary to existing and emerging skill management systems and repositories (e.g., SkillNet, AgentSkillOS). While these platforms focus on the creation, organization, and orchestration of skills at an ecosystem level, GoS provides a critical downstream inference-time layer for selecting the most pertinent executable subset. This suggests a modular future for agent development, where specialized components handle different aspects of skill management.

5.  **Insights into Model Behavior and Context Management:** The ablation studies and performance comparisons offer insights into how different LLM families interact with varying context lengths and structured information. The observation that GoS helps agents commit to verifier-aligned plans earlier, even when relevant skills are present in other baselines, suggests that providing a coherent, dependency-aware context can significantly influence an agent's reasoning and planning efficiency.

6.  **Future Research Directions:** The paper identifies several avenues for future work, including refining graph structure from execution traces, online adaptation of edge weights, stronger reranking mechanisms for candidate bundles, and broader evaluation in multimodal and interactive agent settings. These suggestions highlight the potential for continuous improvement and expansion of the GoS framework.

In conclusion, GoS represents a notable step towards making LLM agents more capable, efficient, and reliable in environments characterized by vast and complex skill libraries. Its impact could be seen in the development of next-generation autonomous agents capable of performing intricate technical tasks with improved accuracy and reduced resource consumption.
overview:
The development of Large Language Model (LLM) agents has progressed from simple text generation to the creation of autonomous systems capable of using external tools, APIs, and "skills" to solve complex tasks. As these systems move from controlled laboratory settings into real-world production environments, they are required to navigate increasingly large libraries containing thousands of potential skills. This scale introduces a significant technical hurdle: how to efficiently identify and retrieve the exact subset of skills needed for a specific task without overwhelming the agent's memory or computational budget.

## The Challenge of Massive Skill Libraries

When an LLM agent is tasked with a problem, such as "Analyze pedestrian traffic from a raw video file," it must select appropriate tools from its library. In a small ecosystem with ten tools, providing the entire list to the agent is feasible. however, modern "skill-centric" architectures aim to maintain libraries with thousands of specialized functions.

![Graph of Skills Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05333v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Comparison between Vanilla full loading, standard Vector-based retrieval, and the Graph of Skills (GoS) approach.*

The "Graph of Skills" (GoS) research addresses two primary bottlenecks that emerge at this scale. The first is context window saturation. Loading every skill into the prompt (a "Vanilla" approach) is computationally expensive, increases latency, and often leads to model "hallucination," where the LLM loses track of relevant details amidst the noise. The second bottleneck is the functional dependency of tools. Many technical tasks require a sequence of operations where the primary solver depends on auxiliary tools (like data formatters or parsers) that may not be semantically similar to the original user query.

## The Limitations of Existing Retrieval Paradigms

Current solutions for skill selection generally fall into two categories, both of which have notable drawbacks:

1.  **Vanilla Skills (Full Loading):** This involves prepending the entire skill set to the context. While it ensures the agent "knows" everything available, the token cost grows linearly with the library size. Large prompts also degrade the reasoning capabilities of many LLMs, making it harder for them to select the right tool for the job.
2.  **Vector-Based Retrieval:** This method uses semantic similarity (dense embeddings) to find skills that "look like" the query. For example, a query about "counting traffic" might retrieve a high-level counter. However, it might miss a necessary "video-to-frame" extractor because the extractor's description doesn't mention "traffic" or "counting." This is known as the **prerequisite gap**.

GoS is designed to close this gap by treating the skill library not as a flat list, but as a structured, directed graph where functional relationships between tools are explicitly mapped and used during retrieval.

## Graph of Skills (GoS): A Structural Approach

GoS introduces an inference-time retrieval layer that ensures the retrieved skills are both relevant and "execution-complete." The system operates in two distinct phases: an offline construction phase and an online retrieval phase.

The framework models the library as a typed directed graph $G = (V, E, w, \phi)$. In this graph, $V$ represents individual skill records, while $E$ represents the connections between them. These connections are categorized into four types to capture the nuance of tool relationships:
- **Dependency:** Indicates that one tool's output is required as another's input.
- **Workflow:** Captures common multi-step sequences.
- **Semantic:** Connects tools that are topically related.
- **Alternative:** Links tools that provide different strategies for the same problem.

## Building the Foundation: Offline Graph Construction

Before any query is processed, GoS processes the skill corpus $C$ to build the graph. This starts with **Skill Normalization**, where raw documentation is parsed into a standardized record. This record includes executable fields like I/O requirements, capability summaries, and entry points.

![GoS Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05333v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The GoS pipeline consisting of Offline Indexing, Graph Construction, and Online Retrieval.*

The most critical part of this phase is **Typed Relation Induction**. The system identifies dependencies ($v_i \to v_j$) by matching the output types of one skill to the input requirements of another. For non-deterministic relations like "workflow" or "alternative," a lightweight LLM pass is used to validate potential connections within a restricted candidate pool, ensuring the graph remains precise and manageable.

## Intelligent Retrieval: The Online Phase

When a user submits a task query $q$, GoS performs structural retrieval to find a compact bundle $B(q) \subseteq V$ that fits within a context budget $\tau$.

### Hybrid Seeding
The process begins with **Hybrid Seeding**. To avoid the pitfalls of purely semantic search, GoS combines a semantic score $s_{\text{sem}_i}(q)$ with a lexical score $s_{\text{lex}_i}(q)$. Lexical search is particularly effective at catching exact keyword matches for technical terms. These scores are combined using a weighting factor $\eta$ to create an initial seed distribution $p$.

### Reverse-Aware Graph Diffusion
The core innovation of GoS is how it expands from these seeds. Using the typed graph, it performs a diffusion process similar to Personalized PageRank (PPR). The system defines a transition operator $T$ that combines forward transitions $T_{\to_r}$ (following dependencies) and reverse transitions $T_{\leftarrow_r}$ (finding prerequisites). The reverse transitions are weighted by $\gamma_r$, allowing the "relevance" to flow from a high-level tool back to the lower-level utilities it needs.

The importance of each skill is updated iteratively through the following equation:

$$
s^{(\ell+1)} = \alpha p + (1-\alpha)T^T s^{(\ell)}
$$

where $\alpha$ is a restart probability that keeps the search anchored to the original query seeds. This process ensures that if a "Traffic Counter" is highly relevant, its "Video Parser" prerequisite also receives a high score, even if the parser itself doesn't match the "traffic" query semantically.

### Reranking and Hydration
After the scores converge to $s^*_i$, a final reranking score $\rho_i(q)$ is calculated. This combines the diffusion result with direct query evidence $m_i(q)$, weighted by $\mu$:

$$
\rho_i(q) = (1-\mu)s^*_i + \mu m_i(q)
$$

The top-ranked skills are "hydrated"—meaning their full documentation and executable paths are packaged—until the context budget $\tau$ is reached.

## Quantitative Performance and Efficiency

The researchers evaluated GoS using two rigorous benchmarks: **SkillsBench** (1,000 real-world technical skills) and **ALFWorld** (sequential decision-making). They tested the system across different LLM families, including Claude, MiniMax, and GPT-5.2 Codex.

The results demonstrated that GoS significantly outperforms both full-loading and vector-only approaches:
- **Success Rate:** Compared to Vanilla full loading, GoS increased the average success reward by approximately 43.6%. It also consistently beat Vector-based retrieval, proving that the structural graph information is vital for task completion.
- **Token Efficiency:** GoS reduced input token usage by roughly 37.8% compared to the Vanilla baseline. Because it only provides the most relevant and necessary tools, it prevents the context window from being filled with irrelevant "noise."
- **Scalability:** As the number of skills in the library increased from 200 to 2,000, the performance of Vanilla loading dropped due to context saturation. In contrast, GoS maintained high accuracy and stable token usage, showing it is better suited for massive toolsets.

## Qualitative Impact and Strategic Decision-Making

Beyond the numbers, GoS changes how agents "think" about a problem. In a case study involving a traffic counting task, the Vanilla agent often got lost in the vast documentation, while the Vector-only agent failed to find the necessary video extraction tools.

GoS, by contrast, provided a "functional bundle." By retrieving the counter and its prerequisites simultaneously, the agent could immediately commit to a viable execution plan. This suggests that structural retrieval doesn't just provide better tools; it provides a better logical framework for the agent to construct its plan, leading to higher reliability in complex, multi-step engineering tasks.

## Conclusion and Broader Implications

Graph of Skills represents a shift in how we approach tool-augmented LLMs. Instead of treating retrieval as a simple search problem, it treats it as a structural assembly problem. By acknowledging that tools exist in an ecosystem of dependencies, GoS allows agents to scale to thousands of skills without the traditional penalties of cost, latency, or reduced reasoning accuracy.

The significance of this work lies in its modularity. It acts as a lightweight, inference-time layer that can be added to existing agent architectures. As we move toward more autonomous systems in specialized domains like software engineering, data science, and robotics, the ability to selectively and accurately retrieve executable tool bundles will be a cornerstone of reliable agent performance. Future work may further enhance this by learning graph structures directly from execution traces, allowing the "Graph of Skills" to evolve and improve as the agent gains experience.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling and leveraging dependency and workflow structures via a graph is crucial for retrieving functionally complete skill bundles, overcoming the 'prerequisite gap' of semantic-only methods.
A hybrid approach combining both semantic and lexical signals for initial skill seeding significantly improves the quality of candidate skills before graph-based expansion.
Reverse-aware graph diffusion effectively propagates relevance from high-level matched skills back to their essential prerequisites, ensuring that necessary but semantically weak skills are included.
originalProblem: Large skill libraries lead to context window saturation, increased token costs, and higher latency for LLM agents when the entire library is loaded.

Purely semantic skill retrieval often suffers from a 'prerequisite gap,' failing to retrieve all functionally necessary skills (e.g., parsers, converters) that are semantically distant from the primary task query.
Agents struggle with multi-step tasks when provided with incomplete or noisy skill sets, leading to lower task completion rates and inefficient planning.
results: GoS consistently achieved the highest average reward across all benchmarks and LLM models, improving average reward by 43.6% compared to the Vanilla Skills baseline and outperforming Vector Skills in every tested scenario.

The method significantly enhanced efficiency, reducing input token usage by 37.8% on average compared to Vanilla Skills while maintaining similar token efficiency to Vector Skills, and also reduced agent runtime in most configurations.

GoS demonstrated improved scalability, with its performance advantage over baselines increasing with skill library size, notably between 500 and 2,000 skills, confirming its effectiveness for large-scale agent skill management.
solution: An offline phase constructs a typed directed graph from the local skill corpus, normalizing skills into nodes and inducing dependency, workflow, semantic, and alternative relations as edges.
An online structural retrieval phase employs hybrid seed retrieval (combining semantic and lexical scores) and reverse-aware typed diffusion on the graph to identify relevant skills.

A budgeted reranking and hydration step materializes a compact, execution-complete skill bundle, prioritizing skills based on combined diffusion and query evidence scores, while adhering to context limits.
summary.summary: Graph of Skills (GoS) improves the performance and efficiency of Large Language Model agents when utilizing extensive skill libraries by implementing a dependency-aware structural retrieval layer. This method consistently yielded a 43.6% higher average reward and 37.8% reduction in input tokens compared to full-loading baselines, effectively overcoming limitations like context window saturation and the 'prerequisite gap' in semantic skill retrieval.

title: Graph of Skills: Dependency-Aware Structural Retrieval for Massive Agent Skills
